what_it_does: RED→GREEN test harness for codeedges.py that exercises a fake source tree to verify direct, cross-file, and same-file code-dependency edges, bounded transitive reach, stale handling for unparseable files, and reuse of the symbols.py identifier scheme.
observations: Uses inline string fixtures (CHAIN_PY, SUITE_PY, SCHED_PY, BROKEN_PY) instead of disk access or mocks
Defines fake_read(repo_path) as a stand-in for repo-relative file reads
Asserts bidirectional edges: depends_on and its inverse depended_by
Asserts codeedges.DEPTH is 2 or 3 and reach() is capped at that depth with an explicit capped report
Expects unparseable broken.py to surface as a stale entry with resolves:false rather than crash or silently skip
Verifies codeedges.symbol_id is exactly symbols.symbol_id to enforce one coordinate system
Checks emitted document shape mirrors code-symbols.json honesty fields: _what, summary, stale, and resolves per edge
standouts: Inline fixture-based integration test with no filesystem or mocks
DEPTH-bounded transitive reach with explicit capped reporting
Fail-loud stale entry for syntax errors
conventions: Top-of-module docstring states run command and behavioral contract
Uses sorted file list and repo-relative paths
Imports sibling symbols module to verify coordinate-system reuse
concerns: Hard-caps DEPTH to 2 or 3 while the chain length is fixed; brittle if DEPTH changes
Tightly couples assertions to the exact code://<file-stem>/<symbol> id format
Tests an implementation constant (DEPTH) rather than purely behavior
No cleanup of sys.path mutation after import setup
notes: A behavioral contract test that stands in for codeedges.py before it exists, using inline fake sources to enforce edge semantics and transparent failure handling.
questions: What is the exact difference between codeedges.reach and codeedges.reach_report?
How does codeedges map nested paths like runtime/suite.py to the suite stem id?
Does codeedges parse only top-level functions/classes or also nested definitions?
What string does the emitted _what field contain?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose (RED→GREEN test for the structural code-dependency graph) matches the inline assertions and fixture design.
apparent_themes: Single coordinate system across symbols and codeedges
Honest reporting of stale and unparseable inputs
intention_signals: It is trying to lock down the contract of a code-dependency graph builder before implementation, emphasizing bidirectional edges, depth-capped reachability, and transparent error surfacing.
novelty: Uses a self-contained fake source tree with a deliberately long call chain to test depth-capped transitive reach, and asserts that the cap is reported rather than silently truncated.
contribution: Provides a concrete, runnable behavioral specification and regression test for codeedges.py, covering inverse edges, cross-file import resolution, self-method resolution, depth-bounded reach with capped reporting, and stale-entry handling for unparseable files.
summary_for_embedding: company/design/_system/test_codeedges.py is an inline-fixture integration test that validates a structural code-dependency graph builder's direct, cross-file, and same-file edges, bounded transitive reach, and fail-loud stale handling.
intention_for_embedding: This file exists to define and verify the expected behavior of codeedges.py against a controlled fake source tree before or alongside its implementation.

symbols.does: Returns source text for a repo-relative path from an inline fake tree.
symbols.description: fake_read is a stand-in for a read-only file accessor used by the test; it looks up the supplied repo_path in the FAKE_TREE dictionary and returns the corresponding source string or None.

symbols.does: Executes assertions that validate codeedges.py behavior against the fake source tree.
symbols.description: run builds a code-dependency graph over the inline fixtures, then asserts direct call edges, cross-file import edges, same-file method edges, depth-bounded transitive reach with capped reporting, stale handling for an unparseable file, and reuse of the symbols.py identifier scheme.
